title: Nouns & Gender
intro: Every Spanish noun is either masculine or feminine — there is no neutral 'it'. This gender affects every article and adjective that goes with the noun.

sections.title: The basic pattern
sections.content:
Most nouns follow this rule:
• Ends in -O → masculine → el libro (the book)
• Ends in -A → feminine → la mesa (the table)
• Ends in -E or consonant → varies → you need to learn the article with the word

sections.label: Famous exceptions — memorise these!
sections.content:
el día (the day) — ends in -a but masculine!
la mano (the hand) — ends in -o but feminine!
Tip: always learn a noun with its article: 'el día', not just 'día'.

questions.explanation: libro ends in -o → masculine → El libro ✓
mesa ends in -a → feminine → La mesa ✓

questions.prompt: 'el día' — día ends in -a but the article is 'el'. So día is:
questions.explanation: día ends in -a but is masculine — a classic exception. el día ✓

questions.prompt: 'la mano' — mano ends in -o but the article is 'la'. So mano is:
questions.explanation: mano ends in -o but is feminine — another famous exception! la mano ✓
Words ending in -ma (from Greek) are masculine: el problema, el mapa, el idioma ✓

questions.prompt: Words ending in -dad (ciudad, verdad, libertad) are always:
questions.explanation: -dad and -tad endings are always feminine: la ciudad, la verdad, la libertad ✓

questions.prompt: Days of the week — lunes, martes, miércoles... are:
questions.explanation: All days of the week are masculine: el lunes, el martes, el domingo...
nación ends in -ción → always feminine → La nación ✓
